content:
# Exodus Chapter 34 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), pp. 104–106; Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 77–78 | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_034_page_001.jpg`, `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_034_page_002.jpg`, `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_034_page_003.jpg`, `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/EXO_034_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/EXO_034_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

### New Tablets — Moses Ascends Alone (vv. 1–4)

**1** YHWH said to Moses, "Chisel out two stone tablets like the first ones, and I will write on them the words that were on the original tablets — the ones you shattered. **2** Be ready by morning. Come up at dawn to Mount Sinai, and present yourself to me there on top of the mountain. **3** No one is to come up with you. No one is even to be seen anywhere on the mountain. Not even the flocks or herds may graze in front of that mountain." **4** So Moses chiseled out two stone tablets like the first ones, and early the next morning he went up Mount Sinai, just as YHWH had commanded him, carrying the two stone tablets in his hands.

---

### YHWH Proclaims His Name — The Thirteen Attributes (vv. 5–9)

**5** Then YHWH descended in the cloud and stood there with him, and he proclaimed the name "YHWH." **6** YHWH passed before him and proclaimed:

> "YHWH! YHWH! — a God who is compassionate and gracious, slow to anger, and overflowing with loyal love and faithfulness, **7** who maintains loyal love to the thousandth generation, who forgives wrongdoing, rebellion, and sin — yet who will by no means leave the guilty unpunished, bringing the consequences of the parents' sin upon the children and grandchildren, to the third and fourth generation."

**8** Moses immediately bowed to the ground and worshiped. **9** He said, "My Lord, if I have truly found favor in your eyes, then please, my Lord, go among us — for this is indeed a stiff-necked people — and forgive our wrongdoing and our sin, and claim us as your own."

---

### The Covenant Declared (vv. 10–11)

**10** YHWH said, "I am now making a covenant. Before all your people I will do wonders that have never been performed in any nation on all the earth, and all the people among whom you live will see the work of YHWH — because what I am about to do with you is an awesome thing. **11** Pay careful attention to what I am commanding you today. I am about to drive out before you the Amorites, the Canaanites, the Hittites, the Perizzites, the Hivites, and the Jebusites.

---

### Warning Against Foreign Alliances and Idolatry (vv. 12–17)

**12** "Be very careful not to make a treaty with the people living in the land where you are going, or they will become a deadly trap among you. **13** Instead, tear down their altars, smash their sacred pillars, and cut down their Asherah poles. **14** You must never bow down to any other god, because YHWH — whose very name is 'Jealous' — is a jealous God. **15** Never make a treaty with the people of the land, because when they prostitute themselves with their gods and sacrifice to them, they will invite you, and you will eat from their sacrifice. **16** Then you will take their daughters as wives for your sons, and when their daughters prostitute themselves with their gods, they will lead your sons to do the same.

**17** "Do not make cast-metal gods for yourselves.

---

### The Festival Calendar and Offerings (vv. 18–26)

**18** "Observe the Festival of Unleavened Bread. For seven days eat bread made without yeast during the appointed time in the month of Aviv, just as I commanded you — because it was in the month of Aviv that you came out of Egypt.

**19** "Every firstborn belongs to me — including every firstborn male among your livestock, whether cattle or sheep. **20** The firstborn of a donkey you must redeem with a lamb, and if you do not redeem it, you must break its neck. Every firstborn among your sons you must redeem. No one is to appear before me empty-handed.

**21** "Six days you are to work, but on the seventh day you must rest — even during plowing season and harvest, you must rest.

**22** "Celebrate the Festival of Weeks with the firstfruits of the wheat harvest, and the Festival of Ingathering at the turn of the year. **23** Three times a year all your men are to appear before the Sovereign YHWH, the God of Israel. **24** For I will dispossess nations before you and expand your territory, and no one will covet your land when you go up to appear before YHWH your God three times a year.

**25** "Do not offer the blood of my sacrifice alongside anything leavened, and do not let the sacrifice of the Passover Festival remain until morning.

**26** "Bring the best of the firstfruits of your soil to the house of YHWH your God. Do not cook a young goat in its mother's milk."

---

### Moses Writes — Forty Days and Forty Nights (vv. 27–28)

**27** Then YHWH said to Moses, "Write down these words, because it is on the basis of these words that I have made a covenant with you and with Israel." **28** Moses was there with YHWH for forty days and forty nights, and he neither ate bread nor drank water. And YHWH wrote on the tablets the words of the covenant — the Ten Words.

---

### Moses Descends — His Face Radiant with Light (vv. 29–35)

**29** When Moses came down from Mount Sinai — with the two tablets of the covenant law in his hands — he did not realize that the skin of his face was radiating light because he had been speaking with YHWH. **30** When Aaron and all the Sons of Israel saw Moses, the skin of his face was shining so brightly that they were afraid to come near him. **31** But Moses called to them, and Aaron and all the leaders of the community came back to him, and Moses spoke with them. **32** After that, all the Sons of Israel came near, and he gave them all the commands that YHWH had spoken to him on Mount Sinai.

**33** When Moses finished speaking with them, he put a veil over his face. **34** But whenever Moses went in before YHWH to speak with him, he would remove the veil until he came back out. Then he would come out and tell the Sons of Israel whatever he had been commanded, **35** and the Sons of Israel would see that the skin of Moses' face was radiant. Then Moses would put the veil back over his face until he went in to speak with YHWH again.

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

### Verses 5–7 — The divine self-proclamation
This passage is the theological heart of the entire Torah. When Moses asked to see YHWH's glory (33:18), YHWH promised to proclaim his name (33:19). Here that promise is fulfilled — and what is revealed is not appearance but character. The Hebrew sequence in vv. 6–7 became known in Jewish tradition as the Thirteen Attributes of Mercy (שְׁלֹשׁ עֶשְׂרֵה מִדּוֹת). The key terms are: רַחוּם (compassionate, from רֶחֶם, "womb" — a visceral, maternal mercy), חַנּוּן (gracious — freely giving what is undeserved), אֶרֶךְ אַפַּיִם (literally "long of nostrils" — slow to anger, patient), רַב־חֶסֶד (abundant in חֶסֶד — loyal love, covenant faithfulness, steadfast kindness), and אֱמֶת (faithfulness, reliability, truth). The LXX renders these with πολυέλεος καὶ ἀληθινός (abundant in mercy and true). "Overflowing with loyal love and faithfulness" captures both the abundance (רַב) and the paired nature of חֶסֶד וֶאֱמֶת, which function as a hendiadys describing God's covenant character as both passionately devoted and utterly reliable.

### Verse 6 — "YHWH! YHWH!"
The doubled divine name (יהוה יהוה) at the opening of the self-proclamation is striking. The Masoretic cantillation marks separate the two with a distinctive disjunctive accent (tifcha on the first, etnachta division following), suggesting they are not simply repetition for emphasis but two distinct declarations. Rabbinic tradition (b. Rosh Hashanah 17b) interpreted the doubling as indicating YHWH is the same God before and after a person sins — the God of mercy before transgression and the God of mercy after repentance. The LXX in Vaticanus renders κύριος κύριος. The dynamic rendering preserves the doubled name as it stands, trusting that its weight is felt.

### Verse 7 — "Yet who will by no means leave the guilty unpunished"
The Hebrew וְנַקֵּה לֹא יְנַקֶּה uses an emphatic infinitive absolute construction: literally "and acquitting — he will not acquit." This is the hinge of the entire proclamation. YHWH's mercy is vast (to the thousandth generation), but it is not indiscriminate; wrongdoing has real consequences. The contrast between "thousandth generation" for loyal love and "third and fourth generation" for consequences reveals the asymmetry: mercy vastly outweighs judgment. The LXX renders this as οὐ καθαριεῖ τὸν ἔνοχον (he will not cleanse the guilty). The phrase "bringing the consequences" is a dynamic rendering of פֹּקֵד (visiting, attending to), which in context means allowing the effects of sin to ripple through subsequent generations — not punishing the innocent, but describing the intergenerational reality of sin's damage.

### Verse 9 — "Claim us as your own"
The Hebrew וּנְחַלְתָּנוּ literally means "and you will inherit us" or "take us as your inheritance/possession." Moses' logic is remarkable: precisely because Israel is stiff-necked, they need YHWH to go among them, forgive them, and take personal ownership of them. A compliant people might manage on their own; this people requires a God who personally stakes his claim on them. The LXX uses ἔσῃ ἡμῶν (you will be ours), inverting the direction. The Hebrew is more profound — YHWH inheriting Israel, making them his treasured possession — and the dynamic rendering "claim us as your own" captures this theology of divine commitment to a difficult people.

### Verse 14 — "YHWH — whose very name is 'Jealous'"
The Hebrew כִּי יְהוָה קַנָּא שְׁמוֹ אֵל קַנָּא הוּא is an extraordinary theological statement: YHWH's name is Jealous (קַנָּא). This is not incidental emotion but essential identity. The word קַנָּא denotes a passionate, exclusive devotion — the same energy that drives a spouse's rightful expectation of fidelity. The LXX renders θεὸς ζηλωτὸν ὄνομα (God whose name is Zealous). Coming immediately after the proclamation of mercy in vv. 6–7, this jealousy is not contradictory but complementary: the God who is infinitely compassionate is also fiercely committed. He will not share his people with rivals, precisely because his love for them is not casual.

### Verse 20 — Firstborn redemption
The law of the firstborn (פֶּטֶר רֶחֶם, "opener of the womb") connects to the Passover narrative: because YHWH struck down the Egyptian firstborn and spared Israel's, every firstborn belongs to him. The donkey, being an unclean animal, cannot be sacrificed and must be redeemed with a lamb — or killed. Human firstborn must always be redeemed. The LXX preserves this structure. This law embeds the exodus memory into the rhythms of daily life — every birth, every flock, recalls the night of liberation.

### Verse 26 — "Do not cook a young goat in its mother's milk"
The Hebrew לֹא־תְבַשֵּׁל גְּדִי בַּחֲלֵב אִמּוֹ appears three times in the Torah (here, Exod 23:19, Deut 14:21). Its original meaning is debated — it may prohibit a specific Canaanite ritual practice, or it may embody a broader ethical principle that the substance meant to nourish life (mother's milk) must not be made the instrument of death. The LXX renders it literally: οὐχ ἑψήσεις ἄρνα ἐν γάλακτι μητρὸς αὐτοῦ. In rabbinic tradition, this verse became the foundation for the entire system of separating meat and dairy. The dynamic rendering preserves the concrete image without interpretive expansion.

### Verse 28 — Who wrote?
The Hebrew of v. 28 presents a well-known ambiguity. The subject of "he wrote" (וַיִּכְתֹּב) could be Moses or YHWH. The preceding verse told Moses to "write down these words" (v. 27), but the conclusion says "he wrote on the tablets the words of the covenant — the Ten Words." Since v. 1 explicitly said YHWH would write on the tablets, and the "Ten Words" (עֲשֶׂרֶת הַדְּבָרִים) are distinguished from "these words" of the covenant stipulations, the most coherent reading is that Moses wrote the covenant stipulations (v. 27) while YHWH wrote the Ten Words on the tablets (v. 28b). The LXX in Vaticanus reads ἔγραψεν without clarifying the subject. The dynamic rendering makes the subject explicit as YHWH for the tablet-writing, following the logic of v. 1.

### Verse 29 — "Radiating light" (קָרַן)
The Hebrew verb קָרַן is derived from קֶרֶן (horn/ray). It means that Moses' face was emitting beams of light — literally "his face was horning" or "his face was raying." The LXX translates δεδόξασται ἡ ὄψις τοῦ χρώματος τοῦ προσώπου αὐτοῦ (the appearance of the skin of his face was glorified), interpreting the phenomenon as divine glory reflected in human skin. Jerome's Latin Vulgate famously rendered this as cornuta (horned), producing centuries of artistic depictions of Moses with horns — including Michelangelo's famous statue. The Hebrew verb, however, unambiguously describes radiance: the face that had been in YHWH's presence now emitted light. "Radiating light" captures the active, visible nature of the phenomenon, and "shining so brightly" in v. 30 conveys the effect it had on onlookers.

### Verses 33–35 — The veil
The Hebrew מַסְוֶה (veil, covering) appears only in this passage in the entire Hebrew Bible. Moses wore it not to shield himself but to shield the people from the radiance. The pattern is significant: Moses removes the veil to speak with YHWH, comes out radiant, delivers the message, then veils himself. Paul in 2 Corinthians 3:13–18 develops an extended midrash on this veil, arguing that it concealed the fading of the glory, and that in Christ the veil is removed. Whatever Paul's later reading, the original text presents the veil as an accommodation — a sign that even mediated divine glory is more than human eyes can comfortably bear.

### Vaticanus Divergences
The LXX in Vaticanus shows several notable readings in this chapter: (1) In v. 6, Vaticanus reads κύριος κύριος ὁ θεός for the divine self-proclamation, adding ὁ θεός where the Hebrew has only the doubled name. (2) In v. 10, Vaticanus uses ἔνδοξα (glorious things) for the "wonders" YHWH will perform, interpreting the Hebrew נִפְלָאֹת as manifestations of glory rather than miraculous acts. (3) In v. 28, as noted above, Vaticanus does not clarify the subject of the writing, leaving the ambiguity unresolved. (4) In v. 29, Vaticanus's rendering δεδόξασται (was glorified) interprets the radiance theologically rather than physically, while the Hebrew קָרַן describes a visible, luminous phenomenon. (5) In v. 24, Vaticanus includes the promise of territorial expansion (πλατυνῶ τὰ ὅριά σου), closely matching the Hebrew אֶת־גְּבֻלֶךָ. In all cases, the Hebrew Masoretic text is followed as the primary text for this translation, with LXX readings noted where they illuminate the interpretive tradition.
